data.title: Concrete Pumping Holdings' Plunge Offers An Opportunity
data.description:
January 14th was not a very pleasant day for shareholders of Concrete Pumping Holdings (BBCP). Shares of the company plunged 9.5% after management announced financial results for the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Even though revenue exceeded analysts’ expectations and earnings per share fell in line with what was anticipated, it's clear that the outlook for the business for 2026 is less-than-ideal. Even though revenue is forecasted at the midpoint to be higher than what the company achieved in 2025, it's not drastically higher. And on top of this, it's looking like profitability will be a bit weaker. This is certainly disappointing, to say the least.

Despite this plunge, I view the company in a favorable light. Back in October of last year, I reaffirmed the business as a ‘strong buy’ candidate. This was based on valuation factors that outweighed declines that the company had seen when it came to revenue, profits, and cash flows. But unfortunately, the market has disagreed with my assessment. The stock is currently down 1.8% since then, while the S&P 500 is up 3.8%. But the good news is that the return disparity is a bit narrower when we look at it from the time I initially upgraded it to a ‘strong buy’ in November of 2024. Since then, shares are up 13.8%. Meanwhile, the market is up 15.5%. This still falls short of my own expectations considering the rating that I assigned the company. But when I look at just how cheap the business is, I believe that keeping it a ‘strong buy’ even in light of recent challenges is the right decision.

The market is underestimating this one

As I mentioned already, the market did not treat Concrete Pumping Holdings very well on January 14th. The stock dropped 9.5% after management announced financial results for the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. To be honest with you, I do understand where the market is coming from here. Revenue, for instance, did fall year over year, contracting from $111.5 million to $108.8 million. This drop was anticipated by the market, though. In fact, revenue ended up being $5.5 million above what analysts were hoping to see. So that is a positive in and of itself.

The company did provide some detail regarding this drop in sales. Specifically, they blamed the decline on a drop in concrete pumping volumes driven by softness in the residential and commercial construction markets. This is not surprising to me. In the chart above, you can see new residential construction data measured by units for the US. Housing completions in the most recent month available, which would be October of last year, came in 15.3% below what was seen in the previous year. Housing starts were down 7.8%. And building permits were down 1.1%.

This can be chalked up to housing affordability issues. In fact, in the chart above, you can see the current gap between median household incomes and the income needed in order to realistically afford the median home in the US. Because of supply chain issues that pushed home prices up, as well as broader inflationary pressures, all on top of years of underbuilding, the average home price ballooned to $405,300 by October of 2025. The median household income amounted to $85,000 on a trailing 12-month basis for them. But in order to afford such a home without being financially strained, the household income needed would be $122,363.

Construction spending is also facing challenges. Take construction spending dedicated to manufacturing activity as an example. In the chart above, you can see how, after the COVID-19 pandemic, there was a surge in investing activity. This can be chalked up to a combination of factors, the most significant of which was certainly government spending initiatives aimed at supporting the economy during a difficult time. However, by August of last year, which is the most recent month for which we have data available, construction spending was down 8.2% year over year. And in fact, you would have to go back to October of 2023 in order to find a month that was worse than August was. The good news is that declining interest rates should prove positive for anything related to construction, whether it be residential or commercial. But the bad news is that economic conditions are weakening because of tariffs and persistently high-interest rates aimed at combating inflation. So while we will eventually see some relief, we should expect to see some additional pain first. Management echoes this, as you will see shortly.

It is worth noting that not all parts of the company performed similarly. In the image above, you can see year over year revenue for each of its operating segments. Because of the aforementioned factors, both the U.S. Concrete Pumping and U.K. Operations segments of the business reported declines in sales. However, the U.S. Concrete Waste Management Services segment did quite well, with revenue growing 7.5% from $19.8 million to $21.3 million. The company’s Eco-Pan brand is doing exceptionally well, with organic revenue growth driven by higher pan pickup volumes and increased prices. This is a market that, just when it comes to the US alone, management believes is worth about $850 million. And with only $75 million worth of revenue being generated by the company on an annual basis from these activities, there is plenty of room for upside. In fact, for the 2016 through 2025 fiscal years, this business line has succeeded in capturing annualized revenue growth of around 15%.

As revenue for the company declined, profitability followed suit. Earnings per share dropped from $0.16 to $0.09, though that was in line with what analysts were hoping to see. This meant a decline in net income from $9 million to $4.9 million. Other profitability metrics took a hit also. Operating cash flow contracted from $22.4 million to $14.4 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a drop from $27.1 million to $23.6 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the business declined from $33.7 million to $30.7 million.

The chart above shows financial results for the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety compared to 2024. Revenue, profits, and cash flows, all fell. And in the image below, you can see the contribution that each operating segment had in pushing sales lower. Unfortunately, management expects a mixed bag when it comes to 2026. The company is planning to invest about $22 million into capital projects that were originally earmarked for 2027 specifically because of worries over stricter NOx emission standards that are expected to be enforced starting in 2027. This essentially refers to nitrogen oxides, which management described as ultimately produced by diesel engines. This will have a big impact on heavy-duty equipment. So to address this concern, the company is making investments in its fleet in order to essentially put more equipment onto the market before that deadline, since those standards only apply to new equipment put into place next year.

Even with these significant expenditures anticipated, management expects revenue to only come in at between $390 million and $410 million. At the midpoint, that's barely better than the $392.9 million the company reported for 2025. But more problematic is the expectation that EBITDA will range between $90 million and $100 million. At the midpoint, that would represent a drop from the $97 million the company reported last year. In all likelihood, this would translate to adjusted operating cash flow of about $70.1 million.

Honestly, I do have some concerns about broader market conditions. Management expects, for instance, for US concrete production to drop in 2026. This would be the 4th consecutive year of declines. From 2022 through 2025, concrete production here at home dropped from 401 million metric tons down to 367 million metric tons. And even though the chart doesn't really relay the data well, we are probably looking at a 2% to 2.5% drop in production this year. In the UK, the picture is different. Construction spending is expected to grow from £106 billion to £111 billion. This excludes infrastructure spending, which is expected to rise from £36 billion to £38 billion. So at least there is some positive data here, in addition to the continued growth associated with its Eco-Pan business line.

If we use the estimates that management provided and that I calculated for the 2026 fiscal year, and we also use the historical results that the company achieved for 2024 and 2025, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. I then, in the chart below, stacked it up against five other similar enterprises. And what I found was that, across the board, our prospect ended up being the cheapest of the group.

This is not the only way to value the company. We can also use the price to book multiple. Right now, the company is trading at 1.34 times book value. As the chart below illustrates, other companies that have similarities to it are trading quite a bit higher than this. The next closest is trading at a multiple of 1.68. And the most expensive shown is trading at a multiple of 4.57. This is most certainly telling. And it indicates that shares are ridiculously cheap.

Shares are so cheap, in fact, that upside potential is massive. In the chart below, I calculated how much upside the stock would experience if the firm were to trade at the same multiple as the cheapest of these five enterprises. We would be looking at an upside of between 29.9% and 100%. That chart also shows how much upside would be captured if the company were to trade at the average multiple of these five businesses, removing, of course, one obvious outlier in the preceding chart. This would imply an upside of between 72.2% and 166.2%.

One thing that you may have noticed is that the upside potential for the company is more limited when we use the EV-to-EBITDA approach. And this does bring us to one weakness of the enterprise. The business currently has net debt of $373.5 million. That's quite substantial for a business with a market capitalization of $354.9 million. And this doesn't even include the $25 million of preferred stock added to the mix. In the chart below, you can see that Concrete Pumping Holdings has a net leverage ratio of 3.85. That is, unfortunately, higher than four of the five institutions that I pointed out as comparable enterprises. This does justify some discount. But even so, the discount as it stands today seems absurd.

Takeaway

Fundamentally speaking, I understand that Concrete Pumping Holdings is enduring some weakness right now. I wouldn't be surprised if 2026 ends up being a bit weaker than what management anticipates. Having said that, even with weakness factored in, shares are trading at low multiples. Yes, net debt is higher than I would like it to be. But it's not so high as to scuttle my overall bullish outlook. It is with that in mind that I believe that maintaining a ‘strong buy’ rating is the right choice.

data.title: Taiwan Semiconductor's Q4 Earnings Should Ignite Rally Continuation
data.description:
TSMC Stock Is On The Rise Thanks To Q4 Beat

The U.S. pre-market session has only recently opened, and what I had initially expected is already taking place: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) (TSMWF) is rising on its Q4 earnings release because a) we see a strong double beat, with EPS beating by over 5%, which is relatively strong for Q4s, and b) their margins remain very strong, with a gross margin of 62.3% (an expansion of 3.3% over Q4 2024).

The financial growth looks massive, with Q4 net income hitting ~$16 billion (+35% YoY), above analysts' views and leading to the 8th consecutive quarter of YoY NI growth. The operating leverage remains at a very solid level because TSMC managed to reach that 35% growth in NI amid sales growth of just 20.5% YoY (or +5.7% QoQ). The Q4 earnings helped TSMC surpass the $100 billion revenue mark for the first time in its corporate history.

Taiwan Semiconductor Still Has Plenty Of Catalysts For Growth

The catalysts that I see for TSMC ahead make it look like a "buy," with unlikely peaking financials at this point in time.

Notably, in Q4, TSM's advanced technologies (7nm and below) accounted for ~77% of wafer revenue, based on the statement, so it shows quite strong demand for cutting-edge process nodes (especially 3nm and 5nm). I see this as the key catalyst for 2026 (and beyond) because 3nm sales are higher-margin in nature, and it's already 28% of total wafer revenue (as of Q4). Next, TSMC is going to transition to even more lucrative (in terms of potential margin expansion) subsegment - 2nm technology, which has already entered high-volume manufacturing in Q4 2025, per the management's commentary.

According to TechNews citing Wei, construction on a third fab is already underway, while the company is seeking permits for a fourth fab and its first advanced packaging facility in the state.

On the other hand, in Taiwan, Wei pointed out that TSMC is planning a multi-phase 2nm wafer fab across its Hsinchu and Kaohsiung Science Parks, with ongoing investments planned for advanced process nodes and advanced packaging facilities.

Source: TrendForce data [January 15th, 2025]

In addition to that, the next iterations - N2P and A16 (featuring Super Power Rail) - are on track for volume production in 2H 2026. Once they come out and reach a meaningful scale, I think these tech developments will offer faster processing and better energy efficiency, and the distance between TSMC and its peers (in terms of pure foundry market share) will only widen further.

In 2025, High-Performance Computing (HPC), which is about AI accelerators and 5G applications, has become the main driver for TSMC's revenues, now accounting for about 55% of the whole revenue structure (as of Q4). Here, I see another catalyst: if the current demand for AI chips and the rising needs for more efficient tech don't subside, HPS is likely to become a 70%+ revenue generator for TSMC, hence lifting its margins well above the historical norms. We can already see that TSMC's Q1 2026 guidance - despite quite large CAPEX planned - doesn't foresee major margin contraction risks. Instead, the margins are projected to rise again.

This tells me that the demand conditions look very supportive and justify heavy investments ahead. By 2029, if AI chips contribute >40% to TSMC's total revenue, their growth will support continued top-line expansion and margin stability, making TSMC a real cash printing machine. The current deterioration in the EPS growth rate in 2026 will likely turn out to be too pessimistic.

The same is true when we look at the current revenue consensus - the post-2027 deterioration doesn't align with what's happening in reality, and the upcoming changes in TSMC's focus (from 5nm/3nm to 2nm, for example).

One more thing that I should discuss is TSMC's CAPEX plan. The management set a record capital budget of $52-56 billion for 2026, which is a massive increase compared to the 2025 plan of ~$40.9 billion. While it looks massive and potentially risky if the end market doesn't grow as expected, I believe TSMC should have done the raise to protect its market standing. The management noted that 70-80% of the new budget will be spent on advanced process technologies, which is logical. Taking into account the nature of CAPEX and the lag before it translates into material results, TSMC should effectively extend its superior business growth phase well beyond 2026-2027. This view contradicts today's consensus, and I think analysts are likely to adjust their estimates upward shortly after Q4 (or after the next quarterly results release).

One more bullish catalyst that I see for TSMC is related to geopolitics. In particular, we know that the U.S. and Taiwan almost reached a trade deal (at least they have a consensus on the main topics):

As Reuters reported, the deal could reduce tariffs to 15% (and commit TSMC to building at least 5 more semiconductor facilities in Arizona, effectively doubling its U.S. footprint). Effectively, if all this materializes, TSMC will sharply reduce its supply chain risks, and it might get rid of the long-term discount in its valuation over time. The forward P/E multiple right now is only 24.67x, while the consensus FY2027 puts the FY2027 P/E below 20x. This is quite cheap in today's market for the ultimate leader of the most important tech niche, in my opinion, especially when we recall the financial strength and all the prospects that I've discussed above.

I think TSMC may beat the consensus FY2027 EPS by at least 5-10%, given its track record and overall AI chip demand. The likely relief in geopolitical risks (thanks to Arizona and US-based CAPEX) should make TSM's P/E stay higher for longer. I'd assume 30x on FY2026 earnings. If so, then TSM's price target arrives at

$13.26 * 1.075 * 30x = 427.63/share (an upside of 30%+)

Source: Oakoff's calculations

The post-earnings rally in TSM stock looks fair, and I think it should keep going, so I'm reiterating my previous "Buy" on TSMC today.

Risks

Since a lot of manufacturers had to switch to high-bandwidth memory (HBM) for AI accelerators because of the ongoing memory chip supply shortage that emerged in 2025, most consumer electronics makers will have to hike prices eventually. This could lead to a massive decline in smartphone shipments in 2026 and push the refresh cycle out by a couple of years. About a third of TSMC's revenue still comes from smartphones, so a hit on that front this year can be quite painful, even if AI demand offsets it largely.

Another risk lies in TSMC's CAPEX, which can lead to some margin compression ahead. I think that investing in high tech is a real necessity, but the ramp of the 2nm node might dilute gross margins in 2H 2026, and the depreciation is expected to increase as well. If TSMC fails to convince its customers to pay a premium for the new node, the margins will likely drop, proving me wrong. Plus, TSM stock might remain trading at a 20-25x earnings multiple, giving close to 0 in potential bullish upside.

Your Takeaway

TSMC's Q4 results looked fantastic if you ask me, and I think the pre-market traders agree with me. I forgot to mention that the management decided to hike dividends by 20% following the latest results, which is a lot when we recall the CAPEX needs for 2026.

In my opinion, TSMC's market share in its niche is very sustainable, and it's unlikely to lose to any of its rivals in the foreseeable future. The idiosyncratic catalysts that I described in today's update make me stay bullish on the stock. I see an upside potential of over 30% from today's stock prices.

Good luck with your investments!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Travere Therapeutics 2026 Outlook: Filspari Momentum, FSGS FDA Delay, And Market Potential
data.description:
Travere Therapeutics 2026 Updated Outlook

Travere Therapeutics (TVTX) has just reported a pretty strong close to 2025. On the update, they focused on robust commercial momentum for Filspari and tagged a couple of key catalysts for 2026. The company posted preliminary fourth-quarter U.S. net product sales of about $127 million, which would bring the FY25 sales up to $410 million. They also managed to end the year with roughly $323 million in cash. Now, the company has actually shown us profitability in some quarters but hasn’t been fully profitable on a full-year basis yet. And based on their operational spend in the first 9 months of 2025, the company has burnt through about $148 million on R&D and $235.5 million on SG&A, which would bring the total spend to $383.6 million for the first three quarters of 2025. So, if we simplify that to a run-rate estimate for a full year, it seems the company is spending around $500 million to $550 million. Based on the current cash balance, it gives a runway of about 6 to 8 months; however, Filspari sales appear to be quite strong at $410 million, so future revenue would extend the runway significantly. There are also some upcoming milestone payments to consider, such as the $40 million from CSL Vifor in 4Q25.

Filspari Sales Leading the Way

If we turn our attention to their main money maker, Filspari, it achieved record demand in IgA nephropathy, with about 908 new patient start forms and a hefty $103 million in 4Q25 sales. However, as you know, Filspari can be both a positive catalyst and a negative one, as was the case with this week's announcement from the FDA, which we’ll get to shortly. Elsewhere, Travere also announced plans to restart the pivotal Phase 3 HARMONY study of pegtibatinase for classical homocystinuria sometime in 1Q26, which should see momentum depending on the results. From the chart below, you can also see just how significant sales of Filspari are to the company. In 4Q25, the drug made up 81% of quarterly revenue, and although the quarter-over-quarter percentage growth is declining slightly, the market still sees a lot of potential in it, as we saw with the sell-off.

FDA Review Extended

As you know, an FDA review getting extended isn’t the best of outcomes; however, it isn’t the worst either. Over the last 3 days, the stock has been down about 16%. So Travere has announced that the FDA has only extended the review of its supplemental New Drug Application for Filspari. The application is for its use in focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS). Just to touch briefly on the difference, Filspari, or sparsentan, is already approved in the U.S. to slow kidney function decline in adults who have primary IgA nephropathy (IgAN). These individuals are at risk for disease progression. On the other hand, the new supplemental New Drug Application is hoping to expand Filspari’s label to also include FSGS, which is a rare and pretty serious kidney disorder for which there are currently no FDA-approved therapies. As you know, extending a label is far easier than developing a new candidate. We often see companies submit similar applications in the hope of an extension. From a business point of view, they are essentially extending their TAM with very little work on their end to alter the product.

So, the FDA has pushed the PDUFA target action date back a bit from January 13th to April 13th this year. It’s a three-month delay that is a result of the company's request to further characterize Filspari’s clinical benefit. The FDA has viewed this as a major amendment. So, the good news is that it’s not from any new concerns related to safety or manufacturing. However, it doesn't mean the application isn’t without risk.

The Risk: Mixed Phase 3 DUPLEX Trial Results

The extension postpones a potential approval, yes; however, for me, it’s also a reflection of the ongoing regulatory engagement around efficacy interpretation. Particularly when you view the application as dependent on the mixed results of the Phase 3 DUPLEX trial. As a quick recap, in the trial for FSGS, Filspari showed a statistically significant benefit on proteinuria reduction, hitting its pre-specified interim endpoint by actually lowering protein leakage in the urine compared with the active control, which was irbesartan. Now, the reduction in proteinuria is very clinically important here because having excess urinary protein is very toxic to the kidneys. It’s actually one of the key drivers of disease progression in FSGS, which makes it a good target to go after, and protein reduction is a meaningful indicator of potential long-term benefit despite.

However, what investors weren’t impressed with was that the study didn’t meet its primary efficacy endpoint of a statistically significant difference in long-term kidney function decline over 108 weeks. That efficacy endpoint refers to how quickly the patient’s kidney function worsens over time. And as you can imagine, it is a very important target. It's recorded via eGFR, or the estimated glomerular filtration rate. That's a standard measure of how well the kidneys can filter blood, and the eGFR slope just tracks the rate of change in this value over months or years. So, in the DUPLEX trial, the goal here was to show that the patients would see a much slower decline in eGFR over 108 weeks compared with those taking irbesartan, which would suggest their kidneys lost function more slowly. So, this is why the results were a bit mixed. Filspari showed some favorable trends, but the difference between treatment groups was not large enough to reach statistical significance. I also don’t see the FDA seeing the trial as definitively proving superior long-term kidney function preservation by this specific measure.

So overall, it’s somewhat of a mixed efficacy picture. On one hand, we have strong early signals on proteinuria, but on the other hand, no clear statistical separation on the longer-term measure of kidney function. In my experience, the FDA often prioritizes the latter for approval decisions, which adds to the risk of buying in now on this dip. Travere, as you would expect, emphasized that Filspari showed clinically meaningful benefits over two years, which, of course, included sustained proteinuria reduction and lower rates of end-stage kidney disease compared to irbesartan. The company is also moving ahead with full commercial launch preparations during the extended three-month review period. So, management clearly has confidence in approval prospects.

A Word on the TAM

Once you get a sense of the potential market for FSGS, you understand why Travere is pushing so hard for this label extension. The market, even though it's quite small, is still a pretty meaningful rare disease opportunity. There are an estimated 40,000 diagnosed patients in the U.S. and a similar prevalence in Europe, which brings the total up to about 80,000-plus patients globally who could be potential candidates for treatment if approved. Based on Travere’s assessment, if there was an approval, the 40,000 patients in the U.S. would form the core TAM for Filspari. Now, if Filspari is priced similarly to other rare kidney disease therapies, at roughly $9,900 per month or about $118,800 per year, the annual revenue per treated patient could hit $120,000. And if we assume modest market penetration rates in the first full-year post‑approval at 20% to 30% of the U.S. FSGS population, it gives Travere about 8,000 to 12,000 treated patients that could see roughly $960 million to $1.44 billion in sales. There would then be additional upside from European and other markets. Keep in mind this is just a rough estimate, and since there are no current FDA-approved therapies for FSGS, it can be difficult to size up the monetary opportunity here.

TVTX Stock Valuation

Travere is currently trading with a market capitalization of about $2.6 billion and $254.5 million in cash. They also have a hefty debt sum of $330 million, which gives them an enterprise value of around $2.68 billion. The company’s valuation, as you would expect, reflects a high-growth, rare-disease profile, with an elevated forward EV to sales ratio of 5.3x based on projected revenue. This figure is sober, the sector median of 3.77. It also tells me investors are paying a slight premium here for its commercial-stage assets like Filspari and the potential FSGS expansion, despite it not yet being approved. Profitability metrics are, of course, not yet meaningful, since the company is still investing heavily in R&D and commercial launch execution.

In terms of stock price, after the latest sell-off, you can pick up shares in the mid-$28 range. Prior to this week, shares were seeing strong momentum and crossing over into $40 territory. So, some will see this dip as a good buying opportunity ahead of the new PDUFA date. We may well see shares spike up with the same momentum over the next three months leading into the decision. Shares are still up about 11% over the last three months, and we also saw a strong rebound when shares hit the $21 range after the news of the delayed review, so there is clearly still some investor confidence priced into the stock.

Looking Ahead

For me, because of the mixed results of the DUPLEX trial, I think it's best to wait until after the decision in April to gauge the upside for Travere. However, if investors see this dip as a buying opportunity and would have more confidence in the upcoming decision, then I see no problem with that. After all, the upside opportunity for Travere should it see a positive decision outcome is huge. Which is why I have gone with a hold on the stock at current levels.

data.title: 2 Of The Best Investment Opportunities I Can Think Of Right Now
data.description:
Introduction

What makes a good stock picker?

Knowing the answer to that question is basically the key to unlimited wealth.

As today’s article is about two of my favorite stocks, I thought it would be educational (and fun!) to look into some examples of successful stock pickers. Thankfully, my life just became a lot easier, as I found a Bloomberg piece on this subject, which discussed how finance experts applied real-life lessons (like games) to their investing approach.

The interesting thing is that “elite investing,” if we can call it that, isn’t that much about predictions but way more about decision-making when dealing with a lot of uncertainty.

That’s why many of these investors like games like poker, chess, crossword puzzles, and Jeopardy! (the article was about how games influence investors). That makes sense, as investing is all about dealing with incomplete information. After all, we can spend 24 hours a day researching something, but we will never ever cover all our bases. There’s always uncertainty.

I believe I have told readers this a while ago, but one thing that got me really into investing is the BBC Show “Million Dollar Traders.” It was a show that was recorded during the Great Financial Crisis, where a number of “regular” people got a million dollars from a hedge fund manager to see how well they would do.

One person, Simon, was a retired engineer. He was one of the people who struggled big time, as he had literally spent his entire career dealing with engineering problems that could be solved by applying logic. On the stock market, that doesn’t always work, as most of you may have figured out already.

Just think about poker (going back to the games). If you’re good at bluffing, you can win with a bad hand. That also means you can lose with a good hand if you’re getting nervous.

And then there’s betting. If you have ever bet on sports (I have), you know that if you see an unusual opportunity, you have to act very fast. Other games, like chess, which may be the most skill-based game, as it’s not about random chances, if you ask me, you’re rewarded for taking the time to think.

Betting games teach players to act fast whenever they see an edge. Chess, by contrast, encourages cool reflection at key moments. As chief economist of the IMF, Rogoff needed that discipline to make policy decisions with incomplete information. The game teaches you how to think methodically and avoid impulsive actions. - Bloomberg

Essentially, we always need to approach this theme from a probability point of view. The most basic way of putting it is by saying that you need to buy great businesses at attractive prices.

The rest will take care of itself. For me, that means businesses with secular growth stories, exceptional capabilities to invest capital, wide moats, and a path to long-term dividend growth. Even if some don’t pay a dividend now, I still want to see a high probability of future dividend growth.

For example, on Tuesday, I wrote an article where I explained how the return on invested capital is more important than the P/E ratio over time, as long as a company has enough room to invest.

Now, in this article, I’ll present two of my favorite stocks, one of which I just made the 16th holding of my dividend (growth) portfolio.

So, let’s look at some real-life examples of high-quality companies that I expect to realistically double over the next 5-ish years.

Miami International Holdings (MIAX)

In an article on December 4, I called this stock a “Typical Leo Stock.” Now, slightly more than a month later, I have bought the stock, making it the 16th holding of my portfolio. Size-wise, it’s my ninth-biggest investment right now, as I deployed a big part of my cash into MIAX.

Needless to say, it’s a high-conviction idea.

For starters, MIAX is an exchange operator like CME Group (CME), which I also own, Cboe Global Markets (CBOE), and Intercontinental Exchange (ICE). The main difference is its size. While most of these trade at market caps above $90 billion, MIAX has a market cap of less than $4 billion.

The company’s biggest benefit may not be size, which is a disadvantage when competing with wide-moat giants like the ones I just mentioned, but its assets. It has licenses, operating exchanges, and advanced technology that allows for very fast execution.

Like its peers, MIAX makes money from transactions based on volume (the higher the volatility, the better), the sale of data, connectivity and access fees, and services on its platform.

As of 3Q25, it generated close to $1 billion in revenues on a nine-month basis. Nearly all of it came from transaction and clearing fees, which means that, unlike ICE, it has a very volume-driven business.

That makes sense, as it started to grow by buying exchanges. Essentially, they buy smaller companies that have high-value licenses (it is very hard to get an exchange license) but operations that are not well run. That’s why they bought the Minneapolis Grain Exchange in 2020. They got the licensing and launched MIAX Futures. It’s a young business with mostly grain-based futures, but it’s a platform that can be used for growth.

What I care about are two things many tend to overlook. One of them is the Bloomberg 500 Index. That’s an alternative to the S&P 500 that’s entirely based on financial numbers instead of a committee that picks new holdings. The benefit is that it’s way more predictable who’s included. MIAX will host the futures and options on this index.

Competing with S&P 500 products is hard. For most, it’s impossible. I did not buy MIAX because I expect this to become a huge disruption. However, I don’t need that to happen. All I need is a bit of a tailwind. For example, we can assume the B500 is cheaper, as MIAX, which has no net debt, can offer products cheaply. Moreover, on its Onyx platform, it has a much younger and faster alternative to CME Globex.

And, even better, Bloomberg is behind it. It has its screens in almost every single financial institution on the planet. This means it can aggressively promote it.

Moreover, because the entire foundation has already been laid for futures, any growth here comes with margins of nearly 100%. That’s one of the other benefits in this industry, as scaling an exchange business isn’t very hard once the infrastructure is up and running.

Additionally, the company sold 90% of its derivatives exchange to Robinhood (HOOD). While that may seem like a mistake, I think it’s genius. MIAXdx (that’s the name of the exchange) has a license. Robinhood needs a license, as it wants to get into “prediction markets.”

MIAX never intended to focus on retail itself. It now has a 10% stake in a business that could see massive growth. Given that Kalshi (one of the two biggest prediction platforms) is worth roughly $11 billion, if Robinhood can create something similar, it would give MIAX an additional $1 billion in assets. That’s more than 25% of its current entire market cap.

With regard to its valuation, the company currently generates roughly $1 billion in annualized revenue. If we see an EBITDA margin improvement from 44% to 50% in the years ahead, that alone would be worth $500 million in EBITDA. At an 18x multiple, which is the industry average, that’s a $9 billion enterprise value. As it does not have net debt, it would imply more than 100% upside based on margin growth alone.

It also excludes any tailwinds from the B-500 business or its deal with Robinhood.

It even excludes organic growth.

While MIAX may have a size disadvantage because most people are not familiar with the company, and because competing with established players is hard, I believe the risk/reward is terrific.

As a result, I put my money where my mouth is.

It doesn’t pay a dividend. However, given the efficiency of the business, I think that changes over the next five years. That’s my educated guess.

If you invest for income, MIAX isn’t right for you. If you are looking for high-quality growth, MIAX may be right for you.

The biggest risk is a steep decline in the market, as I believe this could reduce risk appetite for options, which is a key pillar of income for MIAX. The biggest tailwind is any evidence of B-500 disruption, as this could show the market there’s money to be made in disruption.

Again, this is based on a guess, but if we see market share growth in the S&P 500/B-500 business, I think this stock quickly re-rates much higher.

WaterBridge Infrastructure (WBI)

WBI is another small player without a dividend.

The only reason I do not own WBI is that I own LandBridge (LB), which has the same management team as WBI and its bigger peer, Texas Pacific Land (TPL), which also operates in the Permian Basin.

WaterBridge is an energy company, although not a traditional one. As I have written in a recent article, energy is my favorite place to buy value in this market.

See, WaterBridge went public last year, roughly a year after LandBridge went public. LandBridge was originally founded to own and manage the land that WaterBridge needs for its operations. WaterBridge may not produce oil and gas, but it’s one of the most critical companies in American energy, and I’m serious about that.

For starters, the Permian Basin is the biggest oil basin in the U.S., as it produces more than six million barrels per day. Using the chart below, we see that this is roughly two-thirds of the entire onshore production in the United States.

This production is increasingly constrained by produced water, which is toxic water that is pumped out of the ground as a by-product. In many areas, every barrel of oil comes with up to five barrels of produced water. In some areas, the factor is 10x.

Because of the massive volume that producers need to handle, trucks are no solution. Pipelines are key. WaterBridge has roughly 2,500 miles of pipeline, processing assets, and pore space to pump water back into the ground. WaterBridge makes money from these operations, while LandBridge is paid a royalty for every barrel of water that flows on its land and is pumped into its pore space. It also makes money from surface easements.

Its income mainly comes from produced water handling operations (90% of revenue), which are fixed-fee operations with major oil companies and smaller players that all need someone to get rid of their produced water. These deals have annual inflation escalators.

A few years ago, that wasn’t an issue at all. There was enough pore space, water volumes weren’t high, and costs were low. Now, that’s different, as water volumes have exploded due to higher oil production and deeper wells, which means even if oil production were to remain flat, total water volumes will keep rising, as the data below shows.

As a result, we are looking at a deficit in available pore space, which means pricing power for WaterBridge (and LandBridge), as these companies have available pore spaces and infrastructure projects like Speedway to get water from production areas to this land.

With that said, WBI’s stock price has gone down a lot since its IPO. That’s because of two reasons.

  1. Oil prices have been very weak. Although this isn’t an exact science, as I have written in the aforementioned energy article, I see declining Permian operations as long as oil remains below $65 to $70 WTI. If that happens, growth in water volumes will slow.
  2. The company is expanding through major programs like Speedway. This is CapEx-heavy. Once these projects are finished, it boosts its margins and free cash flow.

By mid-2026, Speedway is expected to be online, according to the company.

With regard to its valuation, the company currently ships 2.6 million barrels of water per day. This year, that number is set to reach 3.0 million barrels due to Speedway. Assuming further Speedway expansions, which are rather “easy” to conduct due to the way Speedway is being constructed, we could see a path to more than 4.0 million barrels of water by 2030.

Assuming that this comes with a rising EBITDA margin toward 56% by 2028, as additional barrels improve utilization rates, we could see close to $490 million in EBITDA. Please note that while I am painting with a broad brush due to commodity price uncertainty, it’s not based on weird theories but actuarial output.

As the company is looking to reduce its leverage to less than 3.0x EBITDA, we could be looking at roughly $1.5 billion in net debt. Right now, it’s $1.4 billion.

Currently, WBI has 43.3 million Class A shares and 80.2 million Class B shares. That’s a market cap of $2.5 billion.

If we apply a conservative 10x multiple to its 2028 EBITDA, we get an Enterprise Value of roughly $5 billion. Deducting $1.5 billion in expected net debt, we get a market value of $3.5 billion, which is 40% above the current price. By 2030, that number could be higher if the market applies a 13x multiple due to higher margins and lower debt.

Moreover, if we assume consistent water growth in the low-double-digit range, that could result in roughly $580 million worth of EBITDA and a $7.5 billion enterprise value. Using the same net debt estimates (assuming no debt repayment), the market cap could rise to $6 billion, which is more than 100% above the current price by 2030.

Again, these are back-of-the-napkin calculations, but they are all based on a likely path forward with a big margin of error, as I am not implying any buybacks, any debt reduction, or any unforeseen tailwinds like deals to supply data centers with water, for example.

While both MIAX and WBI are wrong for income-seeking investors, as they pay no dividend, I think that changes in the years ahead. I also believe that unless oil were to fall off a cliff again and option volumes were to suffer, both companies are in a good spot to realistically double over the next five years. That’s my outlook, anyway.

Takeaway

To me, the main lesson is that the best opportunities are often in overlooked areas where it requires a mix of skill (finding quality), timing (finding the right entry), and even luck (we cannot influence all variables), to unlock value.

Today, I discussed two overlooked stocks that both come with exceptional business models at critical points in the “supply chains” they serve.

I believe that both have a lot of room to grow, attractive multiples, and valuations that truly leave a lot of upside.

They may not be fancy and popular, but I think that will change in the years ahead.

I look forward to reading your opinions!

And, don't go hard on me for not going with dividend stocks here. Those will be on the menu tomorrow.

Risks To My Thesis

I already briefly mentioned it, but in addition to unforeseen risks like market sell-offs and recessions, the biggest risk for MIAX is a decline in option volumes. Moreover, market share losses would be bad news, given its growth focus.

For WBI, the single biggest risk is the oil price. If it remains below $65 WTI (not an exact number) for long, we can expect the market to price in lower growth.

data.title: Zoetis Has Fallen Hard, But The Investment Case Is Still Not Obvious
data.description:
It has been more than four years since I covered Zoetis Inc. (ZTS) in October 2021. I was not particularly interested in the stock, as it was trading at extremely high valuation multiples and the stock price was not justified by the fundamental business. In the conclusion of my last article, I wrote:

We can expect that Zoetis will be able to grow with a high pace (double-digit growth) in the years to come, but I don't think Zoetis can grow enough to justify the current stock price. My assumptions made above (18% growth during the next ten years followed by 6% growth till perpetuity) are theoretical and will never occur in reality. Growth rates will rather decline over time. Nevertheless, I don't think Zoetis can grow with the necessary pace to be fairly valued right now and the stock is not a good investment right now.

Since my last article was published, the stock declined 37% in value. When considering that the S&P 500 increased 57% in the same timeframe, Zoetis can be seen as a terrible investment in the last four years. And since the all-time high, the stock has declined even 48%, making this the steepest decline since Zoetis’ IPO in 2013.

In this article, I will explain why Zoetis is certainly not a “Sell” anymore but also express my caution to label it a “Buy” right now. I will explain why the business has an economic moat around it that is justifying higher growth rates over the long run, and we also look at the growth potential in the next few years. And despite high growth rates, I still don’t see Zoetis as a bargain – however, the stock might be fairly valued.

Valuation Multiples

The biggest reason to be very cautious about Zoetis in the past – and rate the stock as a “Sell” was the extremely high valuation multiples the stock was trading for. In the last few years, the stock was trading for 70 times free cash flow, which is almost never justified for any business.

In the last few years, the valuation multiples got more reasonable, and at the time of writing, Zoetis is trading for 21.4 times earnings as well as 25.4 times free cash flow. Without any doubt, these valuation multiples are more reasonable, but we still need a business with a solid business model and reasonable growth to justify these valuation multiples. We start by looking at the last quarterly results, which were not so great.

Quarterly Results

On November 04, 2025, Zoetis reported its third-quarter results for fiscal 2025, and revenue increased slightly from $2,388 million in Q3/24 to $2,400 million in Q3/25 – resulting in 0.5% year-over-year growth. Operating income also increased slightly (1.7% year-over-year) from $920 million in the same quarter last year to $936 million this quarter. And finally, diluted earnings per share grew from $1.50 in Q3/24 to $1.63 in Q3/25 – resulting in 8.7% year-over-year growth.

When looking at adjusted metrics, the picture is looking a little bit brighter, and when looking at earnings per share over the last decade, Zoetis is growing at a solid pace. However, revenue growth is struggling again and close to zero. Of course, when looking at the time since the IPO, revenue was stagnating in several quarters before, but Zoetis has to grow at a higher pace to justify current valuation multiples.

Growth Stagnating?

Aside from reporting mixed results for the last quarter, Zoetis also lowered its full-year guidance for fiscal 2025, which sent the stock down. Management is now expecting revenue to be in a range of $9,400 million to $9,475 million (and therefore about $50 to $125 million lower than in the previous guidance). Organic operational revenue growth was expected to be in a range of 6.5% to 8% in a previous guidance and was now lowered to a range of 5.5% to 6.5%. Only adjusted diluted earnings per share are still expected to be in a range of $6.30 to $6.40 (the same as in the previous guidance).

Not only has management lowered its guidance for fiscal 2025, but analysts are also getting more pessimistic about Zoetis, and when looking at the earnings revision data, estimates for the next few years are now lowered a little bit. And for the last few years, we can see the earnings estimates being more or less stable – sometimes they were raised a little bit, sometimes they were lowered a little bit.

Nevertheless, analysts are quite optimistic for the years to come and expect earnings per share to grow with a CAGR of 8.1% between fiscal 2025 and fiscal 2032.

We can also make several arguments for Zoetis’ business continuing to grow at a high pace in the years to come. For starters, with an increasing population, the livestock will probably increase as well. During a discussion about the pipeline and strategic vision in December 2025, CEO Kristin Peck stated:

And while the dynamics differ, similarly strong forces of change are transforming the livestock market. As the global population approaches $10 billion by 2050 and the middle class continues to expand, demand for sustainable, nutritious protein will continue to rise.

Additionally, the increasing lifespan of pets is fueling the need for more effective chronic disease treatments. Compared to the 1980s, the average lifespan of a dog increased about 50%, and the average lifespan of a cat increased even 70%.

Management is very confident that it can meet the growing demand in the years to come by a multi-year disruptive innovation roadmap, including several approvals in the United States or a top 10 international market within the next 60 months.

Overall, Zoetis is very confident that the overall market will grow at a high pace in the years to come. Until 2028, Zoetis is expecting a CAGR of 6% for the overall market, and until 2033, management is still expecting the market to grow 4% annually. For 2033, the total expected market might be $85 billion, as a deepening human-animal bond as well as an increasing medicalization and lifespan will lead to a bigger market.

Additionally, we can make the argument that Zoetis might continue to take market share. When looking at the growth rates since 2013, Zoetis has been growing faster than the animal health market in every single year, and the probability is high that Zoetis will continue to outperform.

Of course, we should not be too optimistic for Zoetis and assume that the overall market will grow at a high pace, that Zoetis will outperform the overall market and continue to increase margins at a high pace. Nevertheless, a growth rate in the mid-to-high single digits seems certainly reasonable for Zoetis. We might even assume double-digit growth rates in the years to come, but these assumptions are probably too optimistic to use for an intrinsic value calculation.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

When using a discounted cash flow calculation to determine an intrinsic value for Zoetis, we are once again calculating with a discount rate of 10% (as this is the annual return we like to achieve at least), and we also use the last reported number of diluted shares outstanding (which was 443.2 million). Free cash flow in the last four quarters was $2,240 million, and I think we can use this number as a basis. Free cash flow margin (23.8% right now) is clearly above the long-term average of 16.7%, but since 2018 the reported free cash flow margin was always high and close to the current level. Therefore, I would see the current free cash flow as a reasonable basis.

For the years to come, let’s calculate with a growth rate of 8%, which is in line with analysts’ assumptions, followed by 4% growth till perpetuity. When calculating with these numbers, we get an intrinsic value of $109.88 for Zoetis, and the stock would still be overvalued and not a good investment at this point.

Wide Economic Moat

At this point, we can raise the question of whether high-growth rates might be possible for Zoetis – especially over the long run. A first argument we can make for higher growth rates in the years to come is the high growth rates in the past. Over the last ten years, Zoetis grew its revenue with a CAGR of 6.82%, and operating income increased even with a CAGR of 13.35%. And finally, earnings per share increased with a CAGR of 16.78%.

Additionally, we can raise the question of whether Zoetis might also be able to grow at a higher pace over the long run. In my first calculation I assume 4% growth till perpetuity, which is already high and the upper end of CFI’s terminal growth rate recommendations. Nevertheless, we can make the argument for Zoetis having a wide economic moat – at least the numbers are indicating such. Not only is Zoetis reporting improving margins with very little fluctuation, but return on invested capital is also high and improving over time. In the last 10 years, return on invested capital was 17.65%, and in the last five years, return on invested capital was 19.68%.

In its investor presentations, Zoetis is also pointing out that the company constantly reported higher RoIC metrics than the overall market and the industry (pharma as well as healthcare) – at least since 2019.

In my first article about Zoetis, which was published in February 2021, I already argued for a wide economic moat. Back then I listed several arguments why Zoetis is a “rather moaty” business, although I did not list one single source of a moat. For starters, we can argue that the customer base is very fragmented, which is a huge advantage for Zoetis. Back then I wrote:

Zoetis also has a highly fragmented customer base – a fact, that should be seen as rather positive for the business. (…) However, a large fragmented customer base is seen as rather positive as it usually leads to pricing power. When operating with a fragmented customer base, each individual customer is only responsible for a small part of overall revenue and losing a single customer is not a problem. This gives the company pricing power over its customers.

I also argued for high barriers to entry in the article:

And finally, the industry has high barriers to entry, which will keep new competitors at bay. The investments a new company has to make are rather high – considering capital as well as time. It often takes several years to develop new products. And the fact that the animal health market is rather small (compared to human health), a new competitor might not be able to generate sales with a new product that are high enough to justify the clinical trials, and huge amounts of money necessary for research (not to mention the potential of failing completely).

Aside from the arguments already made in previous articles, I'd like to mention two more points: the first argument is the high spending on research and development. Of course, high expenditures for R&D lower the margins and have a negative impact on the bottom line. But in many cases, high spending can create a strategic advantage for the business that is hard to match for competitors over the long run.

A second argument we can make for a wide economic moat around the business is the fact that Zoetis has become a more integrated partner for veterinarians. Following the acquisition of Abaxis in 2018, Zoetis has its hardware and software directly in clinics, and the software is directly integrated into the daily workflow – making switching more difficult. Additionally, we can argue that veterinarians that are already using the hardware and software of Zoetis might also prescribe a Zoetis treatment.

Intrinsic Value Calculation II

Based on the fact that Zoetis has a wide economic moat around the business, we can also argue for a growth rate of 6% till perpetuity for Zoetis. With the other assumptions being similar to those in my first calculation above, we get an intrinsic value of $145.59 for Zoetis in this scenario, making Zoetis slightly undervalued at this point and probably a reasonable investment.

Conclusion

I certainly don’t see Zoetis as a “Sell” anymore at this point. The valuation multiples contracted over the last few years, and with the stock declining almost 50% from its all-time high, the stock price seems much more reasonable at this point. However, I am still having some doubts about whether Zoetis is already a “Buy” at this point. The growth rates seem reasonable, and we can also argue for a wide economic moat around the business. Nevertheless, we should not ignore that Zoetis is struggling right now and even had to lower its guidance.

data.title: SPIN: The Overlooked 8% Yielding Covered Call ETF Income Investors Are Sleeping On
data.description:
Introduction

With the vast amount of covered call funds that have hit the market in recent years, it's never been easier for investors to create a steady stream of income.

With inflation likely to remain above historical levels for the near to medium term, I believe we will see more funds hit the market in the coming years.

With the vast selection, I can see why it's easy for investors to overlook State Street's (STT) US Equity Premium Income ETF (SPIN). Despite this, SPIN has been quietly performing well and has even outperformed some popular peers since its inception.

In this article, I discuss SPIN's strategy, performance, and why income-focused investors should consider this 8% yielding covered call ETF for their portfolio.

The Need for Income

When I first joined the military at 18 years old, I quickly learned that the need for income was important. This is because I would see so many service members retire and have to go directly back to work.

Part of this was due to their lack of financial literacy and planning. Another was due to inflation, something I wasn't tracking at that age. But we all know that items get more expensive over time.

Insurance, homes, automobiles, etc. Over time, these items get more expensive, thus creating a need for income. Whether this be through dividends or other measures.

And in 2026 and beyond, I think this is more important than ever. The days of a household relying on one source of income are likely long gone. While many Americans can make a decent salary, many still struggle with small items like having an emergency savings.

According to a study, more than 52% of Americans can't cover their monthly expenses. This is why I believe investors should invest for wealth and income and not solely focus on one.

My personal belief is that every investor should have at least two portfolios. One primarily focused on income, and one either a mix of income & high-growth stocks or a portfolio solely focused on growth.

Whether this be through a taxable account or a tax-advantaged one like a Roth IRA. The chart below shows the inflows into covered call ETFs since 2015. You can see starting in 2022 is when inflows really spiked, likely due to inflation.

I don't believe this is a coincidence since the Federal Reserve started raising interest rates in 2022 and hiked them their fastest in history. And although base rates have come down, items are still overly expensive.

But quality dividend-paying stocks or covered call ETFs could be used to alleviate the pain. And here's why I believe SPIN is one income-focused fund investors should consider.

Previous Thesis

I last covered SPIN back in September in an article along with another covered call fund, assigning it a buy rating.

Due to its yield above 8% and ability to potentially preserve investor capital, SPIN was an attractive option for investors looking for a steady stream of income.

Despite their short track record, SPIN's low expense ratio, reputable asset manager in State Street, and solid performance made them a viable option for income seekers.

Strategy

Like many covered call ETFs, SPIN employs a similar strategy to peers like JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI) and Goldman Sachs S&P 500 Premium Income ETF (GPIX) in that it holds actual U.S. equities instead of using a synthetic strategy.

All three use the S&P (SP500) as a benchmark and write options on its underlying holdings to generate additional income. For SPIN, the fund uses an actively managed strategy.

Their investment objective is to provide investors with current income with the potential to see long-term capital appreciation.

Me, personally, I prefer funds that hold actual equities, as I believe they are able to capture more upside from their underlying holdings. Funds like the TappAlpha SPY Growth & Daily Income ETF (TSPY) hold the actual index instead of individual holdings.

Currently, behemoths like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL), and Apple Inc. (AAPL) are the ETF's largest holdings, as Technology stocks, like those in the State Street® Technology Select Sector SPDR® (XLK) make up the largest sector.

Technology accounts for approximately 34% of the portfolio. Mega-cap financial stocks like Visa Inc. (V) and JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) make up the Financial sector — companies like those found in the State Street® Financial Select Sector SPDR® ETF (XLF) — while one of my personal favorite dividend growth stocks, T-Mobile US, Inc. (TMUS), is also in the portfolio.

Communication Services, with stocks like those in the State Street® Communication Services Select Sector SPDR® ETF (XLC), makes up the ETF's third-largest sector. Both Financials & Communications Services account for 13.82% and 11.29% of SPIN's portfolio, respectively.

SPIN holds U.S. large- and mid-cap stocks and looks to hold companies with a market cap north of $2 billion. Management then writes call options, usually month to month, but on those with an expiration date of less than one year.

They also can employ FLEX options, which allows them to customize things like strike price and expiration dates. Management typically selects shorter date options with a strike price close to the current price of the underlying holding.

They also use ladder options, meaning they deploy multiple call options to reduce the risk of single options. This also creates more opportunities to generate income and harvest higher premiums during higher periods of market volatility.

Management will also close options once they're close to their expiration dates, which is known as rolling the options contract. This is when you close the current contract and start a new one with a different date and strike price. Known as rolling up and out.

Performance

When it comes to how SPIN has performed, the ETF has been no slouch. In comparison to some heavy hitters like GPIX, JEPI, and NEOS' S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI), the former has outperformed all but GPIX in price returns.

While higher-yielding funds typically have higher expense ratios closer to 1%, GS has some of the lowest cost structures at 0.29%. Meaning you'd only pay $29 for every $10,000 invested. SPIN beats this with an expense ratio of just 0.25%, the lowest I've personally witnessed. So, despite a short track record, SPIN outperformed both JEPI & SPYI in price returns in the past year.

In total returns, SPYI bested the group, up 16.94% to 16.72% for both SPIN & GPIX. This is slightly higher than the S&P's 16.39% for 2025.

Over the past 6 months, however, SPIN beat the entire peer group and doubled JEPI's total returns in the process.

Since September 2024, SPIN has lagged both GPIX & SPYI but again doubled JEPI, up 22.19%. Again, I believe NEOS and Goldman Sachs are two of the top asset managers today for covered call funds. And their performances speak to this.

The fact that SPIN has managed to not only outperform but also perform similarly also speaks to its quality despite being overshadowed by larger peers. Which should be of no surprise since STT owns the first U.S. ETF, the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY).

And although 2026 could see some volatility due to geopolitical uncertainty, I remain bullish on the index.

And believe we will potentially see another double-digit performance. As a result of the market likely seeing at least returns in the double digits, SPIN should see solid total returns in the 10% to 12% range in 2026.

SPIN's performance can be attributed to their solid NAV growth, something a lot of covered call funds fail to see. Many see NAV erosion, which often leads to deteriorating distributions as well as price underperformance.

But the chart below shows SPIN's NAV has grown nicely since inception.

Distributions and Taxes

Like many covered call funds, SPIN's distributions are a mix of return of capital and net investment income.

Because of this, most would have their taxes deferred, making them advantageous to hold inside a taxable account. Taxes will be deferred until the investor sells or their cost basis reaches zero.

For example, each year the return of capital portion of your distributions lowers your cost basis, carrying your cost basis to subsequent years. Once you sell, your capital gains will be calculated according to your adjusted cost basis.

If you decide to sell before your cost basis reaches zero, you would only be taxed on the capital gain. If you sell after it reaches zero, any future ROC will be taxed at the long-term capital gains rate. This is important to note for when you decide to sell this position.

SPIN also has a lower yield at 8.13%, which also makes them less appealing since peers typically have yields in the double digits. They also pay monthly. And for 2025, the fund paid an average distribution of $0.22.

Because SPIN's distributions also consist of NII, investors may be subject to different taxes. Net investment income is taxed differently depending on your filing status and threshold.

Here are two examples.

Risks and Conclusion

A major risk for SPIN is their smaller size in comparison to peers. SPYI has $7 billion in AUM, while GPIX has close to $3 billion. Despite their solid performance, SPIN only has $56.32 million in AUM, making them less appealing for some.

Something else investors should be aware of is SPIN's distributions have higher variations in comparison to SPYI and GPIX. This could also be a turn-off for some collecting passive income in retirement.

And lastly, if the market experiences a steep crash or correction, this could drag on the fund's NAV, leading to price erosion temporarily.

However, due to their call options strategy that caps their upside, funds like SPIN experience the full impact of market drawdowns. This can also impact total returns.

But due to SPIN's conservative yield, this allows the fund to retain capital instead of paying out most of their income, which also negatively impacts NAV.

On the flip side, distributions would likely increase in market volatility, similar to the months following the Liberation Day sell-off in April of last year.

Furthermore, I remain bullish on the S&P in 2026 due to AI enthusiasm and believe we will see another strong year, up double digits. As a result of this and SPIN's ability to capture the index's upside, I continue to rate the fund a buy.

data.title: Sezzle: Volatility Provides Opportunity In BNPL
data.description:
Sezzle Inc. (SEZL) has experienced a multi-year tailwind from the strong trends across the BNPL (buy now pay later) market. During the COVID period of accelerated spending trends, there were several BNPL players that came to market. At the time, there were high levels of skepticism regarding the durability of these business models. However, over the past few years and buoyed by strong consumer spending, Sezzle has been one of the few BNPL companies that have experienced high levels of success.

Sezzle is a financial technology company that provides BNPL loans to consumers. This gives consumers the ability to split up an expense into installment payments, typically four interest-free installments over a short duration. Sezzle partners with online and in-store retailers, giving consumers increased flexibility. Sezzle has expanded more into subscription services and on-demand financial products (ex: WebBank), diversifying its revenue stream beyond the pure transaction-fee model.

2025 was another roller-coaster year for Sezzle. As seen in the chart above, the volatile swings in stock price have been correlated with the company's earnings reports. The big spike in May 2025 was driven by a very strong Q1 report, which saw revenue grow 120% yoy and material margin improvement. In fact, the company raised the full-year guidance after just one quarter.

However, after a period of bullish momentum that saw shares nearly 4x in just a few months, sentiment became too hot. The Q2 report in August was challenging and caused a near 40% pullback in shares over the following days. High expectations combined with no guidance raise (implied deceleration for the remainder of the year) was a double-edged sword.

A more balanced Q3 report was still met with a negative stock move as sentiment adjusted lower. However, performance over the past few months has been strong, with renewed momentum and interest across BNPL. The consumer remains healthy, and with more optimism across the space, and in particular with Sezzle's competitive positioning, has left many investors encouraged.

My argument from here is quite simple. The business momentum is strong, and the company's financial profile is impressive. Innovation across its product suite is encouraging, and the ramping profitability is helping support a premium valuation. The biggest pushback I have is around the growing bullish momentum, which may cause expectations for the company's upcoming full-year guidance to rise. If Sezzle is not able to produce guidance that is above the "whisper" number, the stock may be prone to another correction.

For now, I am bullish, but would be somewhat hesitant to build out a full position into earnings. With more clarity coming in just a few weeks, fortune may favor the patient.

Bullish Trends and Business Momentum

The company's latest Q3 earnings report in November was more balanced relative to the prior two quarters of volatility. But balanced does not mean bad. In fact, trends were still strong, and profitability improved.

As seen in the chart above, revenue grew 67% yoy, which was a deceleration relative to the 76% yoy growth in Q2, but this was as expected. In my opinion, the two key positives during the quarter were adjusted EBITDA margins expanding ~150 bps and revenue as a percentage of GMV expanding to 11.2%, up from 10.6% in the year-ago quarter. With Sezzle being successful at capturing more of the economics of GMV, this has direct positive impacts to profitability.

When it comes to guidance, there were minimal changes, with the biggest delta coming from changes to discrete tax items. Importantly, Sezzle provided a 2026 EPS baseline target of $4.35, which is a good starting point when looking at valuation -- more on that below.

One aspect of Sezzle's changing business model is its current transition to focus more on subscription revenue. The company has gone back and forth over the years, pivoting their focus between on-demand revenue and subscription revenue. As management noted below, they are more positive on the prospects of subscription revenue.

On-demand has clearly positioned us to be more aggressive with enterprise merchants. However, it didn't deliver like we hoped on conversions at the point of sale or over to subscription. Further, the profit profile for on-demand is less than our premium and anywhere subscription products. We still believe on-demand is a great tool, and it's a great tool to have in our tool belt, but we have adjusted how we go to market with it.

It was probably right around the middle of the quarter [when on-demand was de-emphasized]. At that point, we felt like we had enough data based on what we had been seeing on conversion at point of sale, conversion into subscription. And the bridge just wasn't as strong as we were originally envisioning. Conversions, I think, were slightly better into on-demand at point of sale than they are into subscription, but just not enough to make the payout worthwhile. And so when we started to analyze the lifetime values of the customers, the conversion rates, we really started to realize that on-demand is probably just a better tool around the fringes and at least in the direct-to-consumer portion of our business.

However, it should be noted that going through any type of business model transition is not easy. Internally, this can be challenging from a sales and marketing perspective. And from a customer viewpoint, this could cause some friction at the point of sale, when purchasing decisions are made. Success here is not guaranteed and will be something investors will be closely watching.

As mentioned above, Subscription is a more profitable revenue bucket, which itself is a great reason to make this transition. Sezzle did see strong early success from its on-demand product, but the majority of customers come in the form of subscribers, with the company seeing 10x more orders from subscribers vs. non-subscribers.

Longer-term, I believe this business model transition will be positive, but there could be some challenges along the way. As the company de-emphasizes its on-demand product, there could be some direct negative impacts on revenue, which could distort some of the profitability.

Over the past week, President Trump has discussed the potential 1-year 10% credit card interest rate cap for financial institutions. While the ultimate outcome is uncertain, I believe this could improve the value proposition for BNPL players.

Strong opposition is certain from Wall Street in addition to the credit card companies, which donated heavily to his 2024 campaign and have supported Trump’s second-term agenda. Banks are making the argument that such a plan would most hurt poor people, at a time of economic concern, by curtailing or eliminating credit lines, driving them to high-cost alternatives like payday loans or pawnshops.

The average credit card interest rate is hovering around 24%, and capping this would have direct negative impacts for financial institutions lending to riskier credit profiles. However, these consumers will likely still maintain their spending habits; they will just need to find alternative solutions, such as BNPL.

This news is still developing, and there is no clear outcome; however, the market has already punished the entire financial institution complex given the headline risk.

Valuation

Given the competitive nature of the BNPL industry and the varying growth/profit metrics, there is a wide range of valuations. As expected, the more established, faster growth companies carry premium multiples, while the more speculative plays trade at a discount.

Given the success Sezzle has experienced over the past year, and with the renewed investor optimism about the business, the company currently trades at 17.4x forward EPS.

Sezzle has a strong revenue growth profile, and the transition back to subscription revenue could end up benefiting margins over the long term. The company provided a 2026 EPS target of $4.35, which, absent any material one-time headwinds, appears achievable.

Assuming another strong year of growth combined with margin expansion, there is a path for 2027 EPS to reach $5.25+. I believe this could start to be the baseline of expectations for the stock. This would mean the stock is currently trading at ~14.5x 2027 EPS, which screens attractive given the growth potential here.

With earnings estimates biased higher over the next year, the valuation multiple does not need to move for the stock to keep working.

Risks

In my opinion, the biggest risk to my bull thesis is competition. Although the number of BNPL companies has consolidated over the years, competition is still high. The leading player in the market is Affirm, though several other players, such as Afterpay, Klarna, PayPal, and others, have amassed meaningful market share. With high competition often comes some level of price wars. If Sezzle is not able to innovate its credit solutions that appeal to consumers, competition may take market share.

Consumer spending is another area of risk. If consumer confidence and/or spending were to deteriorate, this would not only result in lower transaction volumes, but also potentially higher credit loss exposure.

Additionally, there are some risks around regulation. Although the market still operates rather freely today, new legislation could prohibit growth. For example, the Trump Administration is looking to cap credit card interest rates at 10%. The indirect impacts on BNPL are yet to be seen, but if legislation were to directly impact the BNPL lending environment, this would be a clear negative.

Final Thoughts

I believe BNPL has carved out a niche within broader consumer spending patterns, and one that is likely to stay long-term. Sure, the market is highly fragmented and competitive, but Sezzle has proven it has some level of staying power. The company has a strong growth story, improving margins, and product innovation that is appealing to consumers.

Although the stock is up over 40% since its November lows, there is still another 2-3x potential from here just to recapture its all-time highs. Is this a guarantee? Of course not. But I believe Sezzle is well positioned to maintain the bullish momentum for 2026.

data.title: Sales Of Existing Homes In 2025 Drop To Lowest Since 1995, Sellers Massively Yank Listings Off The Market, Waiting For Spring
data.description:
Single-family home sales edged up in 2025, condo sales dropped further.

Annual sales of all types of existing homes declined by 0.2% in 2025 from the prior year, the fourth year in a row of declines, to 4.06 million homes, the lowest since 1995, with single-family home sales ticking up a hair and condo and co-op sales dropping further.

Monthly sales that closed in December rose by 1.4% year-over-year, to a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 4.35 million homes, the best December since 2021, but still down by 29% from December 2021, according to the National Association of Realtors today.

For the past three years, home sales have run essentially at the same collapsed pace, down by 34% from the pandemic high of 2021 and down by 43% from the all-time high in 2005 (historical data via YCharts).

Single-family home sales in 2025 ticked up by 0.5% to 3.69 million homes. But that was still down by 32% from the pandemic high in 2021 and down by 40% from the all-time high in 2005.

Monthly sales that closed in December rose by 1.8% year-over-year to a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 3.95 million homes, the best December since 2021, but down by 27% from December 2021.

Condo and Co-op sales in 2025 fell by 4.6% to 373,000, the lowest in the data by NAR going back to 2009, down by 32% from the pandemic high in 2021, and down by 40% from the all-time high in 2021.

But the condo bubble of 2021, when sales exploded to 707,000 and prices exploded in many markets, is now sticking out like a sore thumb. Since then, condo prices have been careening down in many markets and in some markets are down by 30% and more from their peaks in prior years.

Monthly condo sales that closed in December fell by 2.4% year-over-year to a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 400,000 condos, along with December 2023 the worst December on record, and down by 41% from December 2021.

Yanking their listings off the market over the holidays

Supply of homes for sale always plunges in December as sellers yank their listings off the market over the holidays, only to relist them in the spring.

In November, supply of all homes combined had been the highest for any November in many years. But in December, sellers ran out of patience, and the yanking-fest was bigger than in some prior Decembers, especially with condos.
