Supply of single-family homes fell to 3.3 months in December (red line with big squares in the chart below), a steep drop from November (4.0 months), which had been the highest for any November since 2018.

But even the sharply lower supply in December was still the highest for any December since 2018.

In terms of condos, sellers yanked their condos off the market at a stunning pace over the holidays, amid little demand and big haircuts on prices in many markets – to relist them in the spring.

Only 32,000 condos were sold in December, but inventory plunged by 79,000 condos, or by 42.5%, from the prior month. That’s the force of delisting over the holidays.

Supply — which is inventory divided by the sales rate — plunged to 3.2 months from 5.6 months in November, which had been the highest for any November since 2011 during the housing bust.

The national median price of single-family homes fell by 1.3% in December from November along seasonal patterns, to $409,500, just 0.2% above December 2024 ($408,500).

This national median price of single-family homes had exploded by 47% from June 2020 through June 2025, most of it during the two years from mid-2020 to mid-2022.

January or February usually marks the seasonal low each year, and June the seasonal high. The index is not seasonally adjusted. The seasonal zigzag is a result of shifts in the mix of what is on the market and sells, which shifts the median price up or down.

But price dynamics differ dramatically from market to market, and the national median price is meaningless for specific markets. In some markets, home prices have fallen substantially, including by 24% in the Austin-Round Rock-San Marcos metro, the biggest drop in our lineup of 33 large and expensive housing markets. Conversely, the Milwaukee-Waukesha metro is the market with the biggest year-over-year gain (+4.1%) and record prices.

The national median price of condos and co-ops rose by 1.3% in December from November, on dismal sales volume that shifted the mix of what sold. Year-over-year, the median price was up by 1.5%.

This national median price of condos and co-ops had exploded by 43% from mid-2020 through mid-2025, most of it during the two years of mid-2020 to mid-2022.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The One Retirement Portfolio I'd Trust With My Entire Passive Income
data.description:
If I were to retire tomorrow, my primary concern would not be beating the S&P 500 (SPY). Instead, it would be generating a reliable, inflation-resistant income stream that would also be as tax-efficient as possible. Thus, I would be looking to invest in securities that provide the highest and most dependable income streams over the long term and invest in them in such a way that my tax burden would be minimized so that I would get the best possible after-tax passive income stream that should last me through all sorts of economic and market environments.

I thus would want to invest in a portfolio that was inflation- and recession-resistant, because these are the two environments in which passive income streams often fall behind the most, with dividend cuts striking during recessions and inflation eroding purchasing power. Additionally, the tax efficiency component is important because it puts me in the most advantageous position possible to generate attractive passive income over the long term. This includes attacking the tax hit from two different directions.

First of all, minimize exposure to C-corporations that get hit with hefty taxation and are exposed to the potential for corporate income tax rising with shifting political winds. Additionally, approach pass-through entities, which evade the corporate income tax, in such a way that their distributions are as tax-sheltered as possible. Thus, you can ultimately invest in a portfolio of attractive income generators without having to pay much at all in the way of taxes, thereby giving yourself a significant boost to your effective income yield and putting yourself on the path to long-term alpha without even having to make any intelligent investing moves.

In particular, I like to invest in limited partnerships (AMLP), real estate investment trusts (VNQ), and business development companies (BIZD) because these are three forms of publicly traded pass-through entities that tend to pay out very high yields and do not get hit with corporate income taxes. Note that this article is not tax advice, and there may be securities mentioned that issue a K-1 tax form, in particular among LPs. Therefore, you need to be sure to do your own tax due diligence before investing in any of these securities or following any of the strategies mentioned in this article, because it may not be suitable for all investors’ personal circumstances.

What I Would Hold in Taxable Accounts

In my taxable account, I like to hold MLPs because they are most tax-efficient in a taxable account. This is because their distributions are generally classified as return of capital, which means you do not pay any taxes on them until you sell them, unless your cost basis eventually goes to zero. However, you can reinvest a portion of your distributions into buying more units of these businesses, which can help rebuild some of your cost basis, thereby lengthening the time period until you have to pay taxes on distributions.

Additionally, if you hold them until you pass away, their cost basis gets reset to the price at which they are at when they get passed on, thereby making all the passive income you’ve generated from them over the years effectively tax-free. Alternatively, you can donate these units to a nonprofit and not only get your passive income over the years tax-free, but you also get a nice tax write-off up to the value of the units at the time they are donated.

Tax considerations aside, MLPs are also very attractive income vehicles because they generate quite stable cash flows, given that most of them have very low exposure to commodity price volatility and instead generate the vast majority of their cash flows from long-duration contracts, with revenues being based on fees and volumes instead. Moreover, these businesses tend to have well-diversified business models by geographic region and commodity type, which further enhances their cash flow stability.

They also tend to be inflation-resistant due to the inflation-linked escalators on their contracted assets, and the energy sector (XLE) in general tends to benefit from inflation over time. Moreover, blue-chip MLPs like Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), Energy Transfer (ET), and MPLX (MPLX) offer yields in the high single digits, which at the moment is around 7–8%, which are covered comfortably by distributable cash flow. Additionally, they’re backed by investment-grade credit ratings with leverage ratios on the low end of the target ranges for these companies, making them very sound cash flow machines.

Not only that, but they are also growing their distributions at rates that meet or beat inflation over time. Therefore, between the strong balance sheets, highly contracted cash flows, and the inflation protections, these are excellent stagflation-resistant income vehicles that also happen to be very tax-efficient in a taxable account. Note that if you put them in a retirement account, many times they generate UBTI if you cross certain thresholds. Therefore, I would hold them exclusively in a taxable account.

If you felt the need to hold them in an IRA or a 401k, look into an ETF like AMLP or the Global X MLP ETF (MLPA) or a 1099-issuing C-corporation like Enbridge (ENB), though in each of these cases, the holdings would likely have to pay corporate income tax at some point. So that is a major negative.

What I Would Hold in Roth Accounts

Meanwhile, in my Roth accounts, I would want to hold BDCs, REITs, and Brookfield (BAM)(BN) LPs like Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP) and Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP). This is because all of these businesses are suitable for holding in a Roth in that they do not generate UBTI, and they are also pass-through entities. In a taxable account, BDC and REIT distributions are taxable, and Brookfield Infrastructure Partners and Brookfield Renewable Partners often generate taxable income as well, which can raise your tax bill when you file the K-1 that BIP and BEP generate. When held in a Roth, though, all that gets sheltered, and you owe no taxes on their future gains.

BDCs are also very attractive entities for a retirement income portfolio because they tend to generate very high yields, with the likes of Ares Capital Corporation (ARCC), Main Street Capital (MAIN), Capital Southwest Corporation (CSWC), Hercules Capital (HTGC), Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL), Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC), and others generating yields in the range of 7–11% per year while backing those yields with portfolios that primarily consist of senior secured loans, asset-backed loans, and other investments in middle-market businesses.

Moreover, their portfolios are very well diversified and are managed by some of the leading asset management teams in the world, who can access proprietary deal flow, have strong underwriting track records, and actively manage the businesses to help reduce losses in the event that things go poorly. You combine all that with the sky-high yield that these BDCs offer, and these become an attractive component of an income portfolio.

Not only that, but BDCs also generally benefit from rising short-term interest rates since they invest primarily in floating-rate loans that are indexed to short-term interest rates. Therefore, in an environment where the Fed is hiking rates and the economy remains on at least a decent footing, BDCs tend to outperform, as they did from 2022 to 2024, when the Fed began pivoting toward rate cuts.

Meanwhile, REITs are also attractive to hold in a Roth because their dividends are often at least partially classified as ordinary income, though some of them are also often classified as return of capital or have some other tax benefits. Moreover, they’re also attractive income generators because they generally have long-duration leases, tend to be defensive in nature due to the critical nature of real estate to many businesses or individuals who use them for shelter, and also tend to have contractual rent escalators, either CPI-linked or fixed.

Additionally, if you invest in triple-net lease REITs like W. P. Carey (WPC), Realty Income (O), and NNN REIT (NNN), they also tend to be highly recession-resistant. Multifamily REITs like Camden Property Trust (CPT), Mid-America Apartment Communities (MAA), and AvalonBay (AVB) also tend to be at least somewhat recession-resistant due to their critical nature. These types of REITs both tend to have consistent dividend growth track records as well, due to their defensive nature, which makes them good, dependable dividend payers in a retiree’s portfolio.

Meanwhile, they also tend to deliver mid-single-digit yields, which are pretty attractive, especially when considering the defensive nature. However, because they have long-duration leases and are not quite as inflation-resistant as midstream MLPs, they tend to perform best when interest rates are falling and perform worst when interest rates are rising. Therefore, they provide a nice counterbalance to BDCs since they tend to perform better in different environments.

This explains why REITs significantly lagged BDCs between 2022 and mid-2024.

Meanwhile, Brookfield Infrastructure Partners and Brookfield Renewable Partners also tend to outperform when interest rates are falling since they also have long-duration, bond-like cash flow structures. However, they do have highly inflation-linked contracted cash flows that make them pretty good stagflation investments while also providing attractive diversification into other areas of infrastructure, including renewable power, hydropower, nuclear energy, transportation infrastructure, and data centers, while paying out attractive distribution yields of between 5–6%, backed by BBB+ credit ratings and high single-digit to low double-digit annualized growth rates.

How These Assets Balance Each Other Across Market Cycles

While this portfolio will likely experience a lot of volatility in the event of a market crash, outside of that, it should be well diversified because these different types of securities complement each other nicely, with broad diversification and different performances in rising interest rate and falling interest rate environments. Additionally, all of these asset types are quite defensive in nature, meaning that the income stream should be fairly resilient to recessions while also generating a weighted average yield in the high single digits, thereby making them a great fit for a retiree looking to retire on passive income from investments.

Of course, you can further diversify this portfolio by investing in traditional blue-chip dividend growth C-corporations through individual stock picks or even through a diversified ETF like the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD). You can also give yourself exposure to precious metals (GLD) income through either dividend-paying gold mining stocks (GDX) or, even better, gold and silver bullion leases, which tend to have a higher yield and lower volatility and operating risk than gold miners do.

Moreover, you can add in other income funds like PIMCO’s actively managed closed-end funds (PDI)(PDO) that pay out high yields, and you can also even invest a small amount into a covered-call, mega-cap tech-heavy ETF like the NEOs NASDAQ High Income ETF (QQQI) or the JPMorgan NASDAQ Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ), among others.

Ultimately, however, I think the securities discussed in this article create a great backbone for a tax-efficient, relatively defensive, and sustainable high-yield portfolio. As such, they are my leading go-to sectors for positions that I hold in my high-yielding, low-beta, and outperforming portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: TSMC, Lam Research Spark Early Market Rally
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Biggest stock movers Thursday: DELL, LRCX, TSM and more. (00:13) Verizon (VZ) restores service after outage, will give credits to affected customers. (01:28) Bilt introduces three new cards with 10% introductory APR. (02:13)

This is an abridged transcript.

The biggest movers list this morning includes names like Dell Technologies, Nokia, Lam Research and Taiwan Semiconductor. Let’s look at the latter two.

Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) +5% - Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), a major supplier to companies including Nvidia (NVDA) and Apple (AAPL), reported a net profit of NT$506B (US$16B) for the October-December quarter, a 35% surge from a year earlier, better than analysts’ estimates. TSMC also said it plans to boost its capital expenditure budget to $52B-$56B for 2026, up from about $40B last year.

Lam Research (LRCX) +6% - Stifel is forecasting wafer fabrication equipment spending to increase by 10% to 15% in calendar year 2026, which should benefit Teradyne (TER), Lam Research (LRCX) and KLA (KLAC). This equates to a $10B to $15B year-over-year increase, which will be led by advanced foundry/logic and DRAM. The financial firm had expected WFE growth of 7% to 8% in a previous forecast. Teradyne (TER) is up 4% and KLA (KLAC) is up 5% in premarket action.

Verizon (VZ) said that it has restored its network services following a major nationwide outage on Wednesday that affected thousands of customers. Over 171,000 reports were logged on DownDetector, primarily involving loss of mobile signal.

Verizon (VZ) apologized for the inconvenience and reiterated plans to issue account credits directly to impacted customers, with details to follow. No specific cause for the interruption was disclosed.

"Today, we let many of our customers down and for that, we are truly sorry. They expect more from us. We are working non-stop and making progress. Our teams will continue to work through the night until service is restored for all impacted customers. We will make this right - for any customer affected, we will provide account credits and share updates soon," the company said.

Other carriers like AT&T (T) and T-Mobile (TMUS) also reported disruptions, though at significantly lower levels.

Bilt is introducing three new credit cards, under the Bilt Card 2.0 name, that offer a 10% introductory APR for 12 months across all three products. That comes less than a week after President Trump said he plans to place a 10% cap on credit card interest rates for a year.

Bilt originally started as a card that provided rewards to apartment renters. "Today, that promise extends to mortgage payments," the company said. The Bilt 2.0 card is offered in partnership with Cardless, Fidem Financial, and Column N.A.

"Bilt Card 2.0 offers rewards on your rent, rewards on your mortgage, and earns you the most valuable points in the market," said Ankur Jain, founder and CEO of Bilt.

The Bilt Palladium Card, with an annual fee of $495, offers 2x points on everyday spending, 4% back in BiltCash on everyday purchases, and a limited-time 50,000 sign-up bonus. The Bilt Obsidian Card, with a $95 annual fee, offers 3x points on dining and groceries, 2x on travel, 1x on everyday spending, and 4% back in Bilt Cash on everyday spending.

Bilt Blue Card, with no annual fee, offers 1x points on everyday spending and 4% back in Bilt Cash on everyday spending, the company said.

Palladium cardholders get $300 in Bilt Cash on opening an account, Obsidian holders get $200, and Bilt Blue holders get $100 in Bilt Cash.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha

Amazon challenges Saks’ chapter 11 filing, calls equity stake “presumptively worthless”

Trump holds off on critical minerals tariffs

Elon Musk's xAI blocks Grok from undressing images after global backlash

Catalyst watch:

  • Costco (COST) will hold its annual meeting. Shareholders will vote on the usual director elections, auditor ratification, say-on-pay, and at least one shareholder proposal related to ESG/climate issues. There has been speculation that Costco (COST) may announce a special dividend this year.

  • Bank credit card metric reports will be closely watched for signs that consumers are buckling to financial pressure. American Express (AXP), Bank of America (BAC), Citigroup (C), Capital One Financial (COF), Discover Financial (DFS), JPMorgan (JPM), and Synchrony Financial (SYF) are all due to update on credit card net charge-offs and delinquency rates for December.

  • Topgolf Callaway (MODG) will officially revert to its old name, Callaway Golf, following the sale of a controlling stake of the Topgolf business.

U.S. stock index futures ticked higher on Thursday after President Trump said he would delay tariffs on critical minerals and confirmed he has no plans to remove Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell.

Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the green. Crude oil is down 4.3% at $59/barrel. Bitcoin is down 0.1% at $96,000. Gold is down 0.3% at $4,614.

The FTSE 100 is up 0.5% and the DAX is up 0.3%.
